coredata.dc:title: A novel magnetoelastic biosensor consisting of carbon quantum dots/nitrocellulose membranes and NiFe2O4/ polylactic acid based on 3D printing for α2-macroglobulin detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xing
Wang, Zhiwen
Hou, Jianru
Duan, Qianqian
Ji, Jianlong
Ge, Yang
Zhao, Dong
Li, Pengcui
Li, Mingzhen
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: α2-macroglobulin (α2-M) has crucial clinical significance as a potent biomarker for diabetes nephropathy (DN). There is an increasing demand for rapid detection of α2-M. Herein, a magnetoelastic (ME) biosensor with a layered composite structure is developed for α2-M detection. Based on 3D printing, the basal layer of the biosensor is prepared as a grid structure fabricated by polylactic acid (PLA) doped with NiFe2O4. The conductivity of the biosensor was improved significantly due to the addition of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The biological site for capturing α2-M antigen was provided by the carbon quantum dots (CDs) coupled with anti-α-2M (anti-α2-M@CDs) in the nitrocellulose filter (NC) membrane. Meanwhile, the distribution of antibodies on the biosensor surface can be observed more directly due to the fluorescence characteristics of CDs. The biosensor in this work can realize multi-pattern recognition of fluorescent signal and electromagnetic signal. The results show that the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.506 ng/mL in the linear range from 10 ng/mL to 100 µg/mL and the linear equation of fitting curve is: y = 0.21 x - 0.15. The ME biosensors with a simple preparation method have advantages of high sensitivity, good stability and low LOD, showing the great potential for α2-M detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α2-macroglobulin
magnetoelastic biosensor
nitrocellulose (NC) filter membrane
3D printing
carbon quantum dots (CDs)
diabetes nephropathy (DN)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: 3D printing PL A/ NiFe2O4 grid structure
Fabrication of the ME biosensor based on a layered composite structure composed of PLA/NiFe2O4+NC/CDs
Characterization of the NC membrane attached with anti-α2-M@CDs
Characterization of the ME biosensor surface based on PLA/NiFe2O4+CDs/NC
Specificity and stability of the ME biosensor

The authors are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62031022, U21A20353), Key Research and Development Program of Shanxi Province (202202010101004, 202202040201012), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (20210302124164, 20210302124429, 20210302124557, 202103021224081), Natural Science Foundation Project of Shanxi Province (No.20210302123136). The data that support the findings of this study are available upon reasonable request from the authors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular drawing of the CDs coupled with antibody to prepare the anti-α2-M@CDs under EDC/NHS activation.
Schematic diagram of the preparation, modification process of anti-α2-M and detection process of the biosensor.
SEM of the surface (a) and cross section (b) morphology of grid structure.
The TEM images of CDs (a) and anti-α2-M@CDs (b). The FTIR spectrum (c) and fluorescence spectrum (d) of both CDs and anti-α2-M@CDs.
Fluorescent image of CDs solution (a), anti-α2-M@CDs solution (b), blank NC membrane (c) and NC membrane attached with anti-α2-M@CDs (d).

The SEM image of the ME biosensor surface (a), the surface of the ME biosensor modified with anti-α2-M@CD (b) and the surface of biosensor tested α2-M (c). The EDS image of the ME biosensor surface (d), the surface of the ME biosensor modified with anti-α2-M@CD (e) and the surface of biosensor tested α2-M (f).

Piezoresistance curve of the ME biosensor (a); real-time resistance response curves for α2-M detection at different concentrations ranging from 10 ng/mL to 100 µg/mL (b); linear curve: the resistance variation as a function of varying α2-M concentrations. (

Specificity measurement of the biosensor by detecting different substances (a). Stability test of biosensors in 7 days (b).
Comparisons of performances between various methods for α2-M detection.

head.ce:title: A novel magnetoelastic biosensor consisting of carbon quantum dots/nitrocellulose membranes and NiFe2O4/ polylactic acid based on 3D printing for α2-macroglobulin detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mechanical Design manufacture and Automation Major (SFCRS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 5Mechanical Design manufacture and Automation Major (SFCRS), Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: α2-macroglobulin (α2-M) has crucial clinical significance as a potent biomarker for diabetes nephropathy (DN). There is an increasing demand for rapid detection of α2-M. Herein, a magnetoelastic (ME) biosensor with a layered composite structure is developed for α2-M detection. Based on 3D printing, the basal layer of the biosensor is prepared as a grid structure fabricated by polylactic acid (PLA) doped with NiFe2O4. The conductivity of the biosensor was improved significantly due to the addition of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The biological site for capturing α2-M antigen was provided by the carbon quantum dots (CDs) coupled with anti-α-2M (anti-α2-M@CDs) in the nitrocellulose filter (NC) membrane. Meanwhile, the distribution of antibodies on the biosensor surface can be observed more directly due to the fluorescence characteristics of CDs. The biosensor in this work can realize multi-pattern recognition of fluorescent signal and electromagnetic signal. The results show that the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.506 ng/mL in the linear range from 10 ng/mL to 100 µg/mL and the linear equation of fitting curve is: y = 0.21 x - 0.15. The ME biosensors with a simple preparation method have advantages of high sensitivity, good stability and low LOD, showing the great potential for α2-M detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes nephropathy (DN) refers to glomerulosclerosis caused by diabetes microvascular disease, also known as diabetes glomerulosclerosis. According to epidemiological statistics, it is estimated that the number of diabetes patients worldwide will reach 366 million by 2030, and the number of diabetes nephropathy patients will exceed to 100 million. DN is not only one of the common complications of diabetes, but also plays an important role in nephropathy. The pathogenesis of diabetes nephropathy is very complex, and there is no clear pathogenesis yet. So early diagnosis of DN is very important. Studies have shown that DN is closely related to the concentration of α2-macroglobulin (α2-M). The abnormal metabolism of the α2-M has a direct role in accelerating the development of DN, and it is also an important indicator for the diagnosis of DN [1].

α2-M is a high molecular weight glycoprotein, which is synthesized by hepatocytes and monocyte-macrophage system. They participate in many important physiological and pathological processes. A large number of studies have proved that α2-M plays an important role in the diagnosis of DN [2]. Therefore, α2-M is considered as one of the most important markers for the diagnosis of DN. Based on current detection technologies, the main methods for α2-M detection are rocket electrophoresis method [3], rate scattering turbidity method [4], and enzyme linked immunosorbent assay [5], which have shortcomings such as operational difficulties, poor repeatability, long detection time, and prone to false results. There is an urgent need for a new detection method to achieve highly sensitive detection of α2-M.

3D printing has shown outstanding advantages in micro and nano processing. It is extensively utilized in instruments fabrication, biomedical, aerospace, and other sectors [6-10] due to its benefits such as strong processing arbitrariness, high molding accuracy and quick manufacturing cycle. Yet at the moment, the majority of the wires used in FDM 3D printers are regular, single materials that can only print the necessary structure and have no electromagnetic properties. At present, 3D printing uses a single filament such as PLA, PPS, PEEK, etc., and no other substances have been added to the filament. As a result, further modifications to the printed structure are challenging. In this research, based on 3D printing, wire made from the magnetoelastic (ME) material of NiFe2O4 combined with PLA is used to prepare the grid structure as basal layer. This gives the biosensor outstanding electromagnetic properties and allows it to sensitively transform the stress signal into electromagnetic signals, based on the ME effect.

Most of the existing studies on ME sensors are based on the rigid substrate of metglas 2826MB. The preparation process of ME sensors based on rigid substrate requires expensive large-scale instruments, and there are some problems such as non-renewable, difficult degradation, poor flexibility, and complex preparation process. Besides, the detection method is single and the anti-interference ability is poor. Presently, nitrocellulose (NC) membrane as a promising substrate material has been utilized to fabricate various biosensors owing to good flexibility, excellent optical properties and biocompatible. Cai et al. [11] prepared a biosensor for the rapid detection of Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Insertion/Deletion (ACE I/D), utilizing the optical properties of the NC membrane to facilitate the observation of color changes caused by different primers. Stanley et al. [12] detected biomarkers by using NC membrane, which were biocompatible and applied to undamaged skin surfaces. Therefore, in this paper, NC membrane doped with carbon quantum dots (CDs) is used to prepare the above layer of the ME biosensor.

Due to the advantages of CDs, such as small particle size, good water solubility, excellent fluorescence performance and strong photostability [12], CDs can replace traditional semiconductor quantum dots as a new environment-friendly biomarker reagent. The surface of CDs contains a large number of oxygen-containing groups. It is the existence of these functional groups that make the CDs solution stable at room temperature, and the rich amino group on the surface can be coupled with the carboxyl group on the antibody. In addition, researchers have also applied CDs to optoelectronic devices, drug transport, immunoassay and other fields. With the continuous in-depth study of CDs, CDs with many excellent properties will play a greater role in the future.

In this work, based on the grid-like multilayer composite structure and 3D printing technology, PLA/ NiFe2O4 + NC/CDs flexible magnetoelastic biosensors were fabricated for α2-M detection. This biosensor is designed by layered composite structure composed of PLA/NiFe2O4+CDs/NC. The PLA/NiFe2O4 substrate layer of grid structure which can effectively improve the sensitivity of the biosensor made by fused deposition modeling (FDM) 3D printer. In addition, adding NiFe2O4 into the PLA can enable the biosensor with ME characteristics and realize the conversion of biological signal to magnetic signal. The above layer is NC membrane doped with CDs coupled with anti-α2-M (anti-α2-M@CDs). Compared with the traditional method of antibodies modification based on Au-S bond, this method can avoid the problem of gold layer falling from the biosensor surface. The distribution of anti-α2-M@CDs on the NC membrane can be observed directly through the fluorescence characteristics of CDs. Compared with current methods, the ME biosensor combined with the ME effect of NiFe2O4, the good conductivity of MWCNTs, the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, the flexibility and biocompatibility of NC film, can realize the multi-pattern recognition of the signal. Moreover, the ME biosensor is simple to construct and low-cost, with high specificity and stability. It is critical for the rapid detection of α2-M and is of great significance for the diagnosis DN.

α2-M and anti-α2-M were purchased from Shanghai Haling Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, CAS: 25952-53-8), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS, CAS: 6066-82-6), phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 M, pH = 7.4) and bovine serum albumin (BSA, 0.1 %, CAS: 9048-46-8) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (Saint Louis, MO, USA). MWCNTs with diameter of 4-6 nm, length of 10-20 µm and purity of >98 wt% (Chengdu organic chemistry co., Ltd, Chinese academy of sciences zhongke times nanometer). The NiFe2O4 were purchased from Dongguan Sat Nano Technology Material Co., Ltd. The CDs were purchased from Green Science Alliance Co., Ltd. The LA were purchased from Innonyx International Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiments was obtained from an ultra-pure water manufacturing system (URT-11-10T). The α2-M antigen solution, lysozyme (LYZ), human serum albumin (HSA), myoglobin (MB), and c-reactive protein (CRP) come from Shandong Landu Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shandong, China).

Digital multimeter (Keithley 2400, Tektronix, Shanghai, CHN) was served to measure the electrical properties of the biosensors. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, GeminiSEM 300, GER) was employed to observe the surface topographies of the biosensors. Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, SU3500-EDS, Hitachi, Japan.) was served to detect the elements of the biosensors surface. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Scientific, Talos F200X) for TEM imaging. Integrated physical property measurement system (PPMS, QUANTUM Scientific Instrument Trading (Beijing) Co., LTD) was used to measure hysteresis loop. Enzyme-labeled instrument (Multiskan FCe, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc) was used to detect fluorescence spectrum. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were conducted using a Fourier Transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker Optik GmbH Tensor 27-FTIR, Ettlingen, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D printing PL A/ NiFe2O4 grid structure
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the PLA was heated to 210°C, and then NiFe2O4 powder was added to the melted PLA with a 1:4 wt ratio under continuous stirring, and then the wire suitable for 3D printing with a diameter of 1.75mm was extruded by the extrusion machine. Then, put the prepared wire into the 3D printer and print out the required grid structure, as shown in Fig. 2.

The electrical conductivity for electrical tests and sensitivity of the biosensor will be enhanced due to the adding of the MWCNTs. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 2. Firstly, 0.1 g MWCNTs is mixed with 20 mL absolute ethanol solution, and disperse them by sonicating for 20 mins to prepare MWCNTs dispersions. Then put the grid structure obtained by 3D printing into MWCNTs dispersions for 20 min and then dry it in the room temperature [13,14].

CDs was coupled with antibody to prepare the anti-α2-M@CDs: firstly, diluted the antibody solution with PBS solution to obtain appropriate concentrations of antibody. At 37°C, mixed the anti-α2-M solution with the solution containing 4 mg/mL of EDC and 4 mg/mL of NHS for 30 min to activate the carboxyl group in the antibody into NHS ester, so that it can combine with the amino group on the surface of CDs more efficiently, as shown in Fig. 1 . After that, dropped the same volume of CDs solution into the activated antibody solution by stir for 30 min at 37°C to obtain anti-α2-M@CDs [15].

NC membrane is widely used in protein imprinting and has strong ability of binding to protein. In this study, NC membrane is used as the substrate of the biosensor for attaching anti-α2-M@CDs. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 2 . Firstly, NC membrane was cut into a square of 4.5 mm × 4.5 mm, then it will be pasted on one side of the grid structure. Then the grid structure pasted with NC membrane is immersed in anhydrous ethanol and taken out until NC membrane become transparent. Finally, carefully clamp out the grid structure pasted with NC membrane with tweezers, place it in a dry container and leave it at room temperature for totally dry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ME biosensor based on a layered composite structure composed of PLA/NiFe2O4+NC/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the biosensor is shown in Fig. 2. Firstly, the grid structure prepared above is immersed in anti-α2-M@CDs solution and placed for 1 h at 37 °C, so that the antibody can be fully modified on the surface of NC membrane pasted on the grid structure surface. Then the grid structure is taken out from the solution and washed by PBS for three times to remove the antibody physically adsorbed on the surface. Finally, the grid structure is soaked in BSA solution in 37 °C for 0.5h and then dried in drying oven. So far, the ME biosensor based on a layered composite structure composed of PLA/NiFe2O4+NC/CDs has been prepared successfully.

The surface and cross section morphology of the grid structure were characterized and shown in Fig. 3 using scanning electron microscope (SEM). The grid pattern on the surface of the grid structure can be seen from Fig. 3a and the size of the square grid pores is roughly 0.5mm × 0.5mm, which is compatible with the original concept of our design. The gap inside the structure can be seen from Fig. 3b. We can see clearly the structure made by 3D printing has the multi-layer network structure.

The CDs and anti-α2-M@CDs were characterized by using the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM). As can be seen from Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b, the diameter of CDs increases from 80nm to 1200nm before and after being coupled with antibody. Fig. 4c shows the FTIR spectra of CDs and anti-α2-M@CDs. Compared to CDs, the FTIR spectra of anti-α2-M@CDs shows new peaks occurred at 1693 cm-1 and 1057 cm-1 which are related to amide bond (-CO-NH-) and C-O-C group, and the peak in 3334 cm-1 is related to OH group. Fluorescence spectrum of CDs and anti-α2-M@CDs are shown in Fig. 4d, after connected with antibody, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases when the excitation wavelength is 520nm. The fluorescence intensity of CDs is slightly weakened, but it does not affect the luminescent characteristics of the CDs. Therefore, the preparation of anti-α2-M@CDs was proven to be successful by TEM, FTIR and fluorescence spectrum [15-17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the NC membrane attached with anti-α2-M@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to more intuitively observe the distribution of anti-α2-M@CDs on the surface of the NC membrane, the fluorescent of the NC membrane characteristic was measured by enzyme-labeled instrument and shown in Fig. 5 [18]. Fig. 5a shows the fluorescent image of the CDs in solution, it can be seen that the CDs are equally scattered in the solution and emit green fluorescence. The fluorescent image of anti-α2-M@CDs solution has been shown in Fig. 5b. Since the CDs solution is diluted after adding the antibody solution, the luminous point was reduced. However, we can still see that anti-α2-M@CDs is emitting green fluorescence. Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d are the fluorescent images of blank NC membrane and NC membrane attached with anti-α2-M@CDs. Comparing the two images, we can see that anti-α2-M@CDs are successfully and uniformly attached to the NC membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the ME biosensor surface based on PLA/NiFe2O4+CDs/NC
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM image and the EDS image of the NC membrane attached on the surface of the ME biosensor were evaluated. Fig. 6 a shows SEM of the blank NC membrane. Compared with Fig. 6a, more agglomerates can be seen in Fig. 6b, which indicates that the anti-α2-M@CDs are successfully attached to the surface of the NC membrane. At the same magnification, it can be seen that the agglomerate in Fig. 6c is even larger than that in Fig. 6b, which is owing to the macromolecular compounds formed by specific binding of antigen and antibody.

For further demonstration of the surface modification process of the biosensors, the EDS characterization of the biosensor's surfaces during the detection process were shown in Fig. 6d-f. From Fig. 4e, the amount of C, N and O in the antibody-modified biosensor is higher than that in the non-antibody-modified biosensor (Fig. 6d), which indicates that the biosensor surface is modified with the antibody successfully. As shown in Fig. 6f, the amounts of C, N and O were even higher than those in that only modified with the antibody, because the immune complex caused by the antigen binding with the antibody belongs to protein molecules, consisting of C, O and. These results indicated that anti-α2-M is successfully modified on the surface of the biosensor and captured α2-M antigen.

The piezoresistance curve of the biosensor is shown in Fig. 7 a, which shows the real time response of the resistance when the stress gradually increase [15,16]. When the biosensor is under compressive stress, more MWCNTs distribute across the voids of the grid structure will connect to each other and conductive pathways increase, resulting in a prominent decrease in the resistance. On the contrary, if tension is applied to the biosensor surface, the MWCNTs will disconnect from each other and the conductive pathways will decrease, resulting in an increase in the resistance.

Fig. 7b shows the resistance variation of the biosensor to different concentrations (10 ng/mL, 100 ng/mL, 1 µg/mL, 10 µg/mL, 100 µg/mL) of α2-M within 2 h. 10 µl α2-M antigen solution is dropped to the biosensor surface and the real time resistance response of biosensor are tested. We can see from Fig. 7b that the resistance is increasing as time goes by. That is because the specific binding of α2-M and anti-α2-M lead to changes of protein conformation and surface stress, then causes the change in resistance. Moreover, the extent of the change of resistance gradually increases with the increase of antigen concentration. This is because when the concentration of antigen increases, the binding sites increases, then the stress generated by the specific binding of antibody and antigen increases, resulting in more significant changes in resistance.

Fig. 7c shows the relationship between resistance variation and the logarithm of α2-M antigen concentration. The experiment was repeated three times for each concentration at the same conditions. It can be seen that the results for each concentration show few differences, which proves that the biosensor has better repeatability. The results demonstrate that the amplitude of resistance change also increase as the α2-M concentration increase in the range of 10ng/mL to 100 µg/mL. The linear fitting relationship of the logarithm of α2-M antigen concentration and resistance variation is: y = 150493 x + 66.523, where x is the logarithm of α2-M antigen concentration and y is resistance variation. And the LOD is 0.506ng/mL.

Fig. 8 a shows that the hysteresis loop of the biosensor for α2-M detection at concentrations ranging from 10 ng/mL to 100 µg/mL. Under the same magnetic field intensity, the magnetization intensity of the ME biosensor after the specific binding of antibody and antigen is higher. When the antibody on the biosensor's surface binds to the antigen, the surface stress changes. Based on the magnetoelastic effect, the saturated magnetic permeability (µ) of the biosensor increase accordingly to the change of the surface stress. The corresponding shift in saturated magnetic permeability (∆µ) is obtained by subtracting the µ of biosensors with different concentrations from the µ of biosensors with undetected α2-M. Fig. 8b shows an excellent linear correlation between ∆µ of the ME biosensor and α2-M concentration over the range of 10 ng/mL to 100 µg/mL. By linear fitting, the linear equation of fitting curve is: y = 0.21 x - 0.15.

The comparison of the biosensors in this work and other methods previously reported are shown in Table 1 . Colorimetric detection and direct electrochemistry by solid electrodes have wider linear ranges, but they have some shortcomings such as taking time or low accuracy. However, the ME biosensor in this work has a smaller size, higher sensitivity and lower detection limit. The biosensor in this work exhibits great potential for detecting α2-M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the ME biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the biosensor's selectivity, the same concentration (50ng/mL) of α2-M antigen solution, LYZ, HSA, myoglobin MB, CRP are detected and every experiment is repeated three times at the same conditions. In Fig. 9 a, the resistance variation of biosensor is clearly enhanced due to the specific combination. And the response caused by physical adsorption between other substances are weak. Moreover, we evaluate the resistance fluctuation of the mixture of the 5 substances mentioned above, and the biosensor's resistance still varied greatly. In conclusion, the biosensor has a high specificity for α2-M detection.

Fig. 9b depicts the test results of biosensor's stability, which is another essential aim of the biosensor. Seven biosensors are made in advance and stored at 4°C under the same circumstances. Experiments are tested at the same time and under the same conditions. Results in Fig. 9b reveal that the resistance variation of the biosensors for detecting α2-M is steady in each test on a daily basis, and that the biosensor's stability is good with a low relative standard deviation (RSD = 6.504%). Given the above facts, the biosensor with a dependable stability has a promising capability in detecting α2-M [15-17].

In conclusion, the ME biosensor based on layered composite structure composed of PLA/NiFe2O4+CDs/NC is first reported for α2-M detection. The CDs connected with antibodies are successfully prepared for more intuitive observation. Based on magnetoelastic effect, NiFe2O4 and MWCNTs are employed to magnify the NC membrane deformation and realize the coupling of magnetic, surface stress and electricity. The biosensor is fabricated by 3D printing, which is a low-cost method and makes control of shape more precise and EDS, SEM, TEM, FTIR and fluorescence spectrum are used to confirm the fabrication of biosensor is successful. Besides, the ME biosensor exhibits high specificity and stability for α2-M detection. The sensor prepared in this paper has the advantages of low cost, low detection limit and rapid detection, and has great potential in the diagnosis of α2-M-related diseases. In order to make the sensor prepared in this paper can be better applied in clinical experiments. Future work will focus on the optimization of the biosensor design and its application to real serum samples collected in the hospital. The sensor is expected to be developed into a portable health diagnostic device.

Xing Guo: Writing – review & editing. Zhiwen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Jianru Hou: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Qianqian Duan: Writing – review & editing. Jianlong Ji: Writing – review & editing. Yang Ge: Writing – review & editing. Dong Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Pengcui Li: Writing – review & editing. Mingzhen Li: Writing – review & editing. Shengbo Sang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no financial or commercial conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62031022, U21A20353), Key Research and Development Program of Shanxi Province (202202010101004, 202202040201012), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (20210302124164, 20210302124429, 20210302124557, 202103021224081), Natural Science Foundation Project of Shanxi Province (No.20210302123136).
The data that support the findings of this study are available upon reasonable request from the authors.
